title: Today's Tech Brief: AI is blowing up music. How should the Grammys handle it?
summary: AI-generated music is shaking up the industry, and the Grammys are faced with a new challenge. Microsoft is also making big moves with AI and Windows improvements.
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for AI is blowing up music. How should the Grammys handle it?
imageCaption: AI is blowing up music. How should the Grammys handle it? (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm intrigued by the impact of AI-generated music on the Grammys. The Recording Academy CEO, Harvey Mason Jr., is grappling with how to handle AI-created music in the age of human creativity. It's a tough call, as AI-generated music is becoming increasingly sophisticated and blurring the lines between human and machine creativity. I mean, who gets the Grammy when a song is written by a human and an AI? It's a conundrum that's got everyone in the music industry scratching their heads.

Next up, Microsoft is making some big announcements at its Build conference. The company is unveiling new AI models, including a Copilot super app and a new reasoning AI model. These tools are designed to make Windows more intuitive and user-friendly. I'm excited to see how these new features will change the game for developers and consumers alike. It's clear that Microsoft is trying to stay competitive in the AI space and make its products more appealing.

Another interesting tidbit from The Verge: Microsoft is also working on improving Windows developer mode. This could make it easier for developers to create apps and integrate AI into their products. I'm looking forward to seeing what kind of innovative apps and services come out of this. It's all about making the platform more attractive to developers and users alike.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
